subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner analyses linear relationships to solve geometric problems and interpret spatial relationships in real-life situations.

lessons.title: The Cartesian Plane ŌĆö Gradient, Distance, and Midpoint of a Line

content.body: Coordinate geometry ŌĆö also called analytic geometry or Cartesian geometry ŌĆö is the branch of mathematics that uses algebraic methods to study geometric shapes and their properties. By placing geometric objects on a coordinate plane (the Cartesian plane), we can use algebra to find lengths, angles, positions, and relationships precisely. This is the foundation for engineering design, GPS navigation, satellite mapping, construction, and data visualisation. In Uganda, coordinate geometry underpins surveying land boundaries, planning road networks, and designing buildings.
The Cartesian Coordinate System

items: The Cartesian plane is formed by two perpendicular number lines: the horizontal x-axis and the vertical y-axis, intersecting at the origin O(0, 0).

Every point P in the plane is uniquely described by an ordered pair (x, y), called its coordinates. x is the horizontal displacement from the origin (positive right, negative left). y is the vertical displacement (positive up, negative down).
The four quadrants: Quadrant I (x>0, y>0), Quadrant II (x<0, y>0), Quadrant III (x<0, y<0), Quadrant IV (x>0, y<0).
Example: Point A(3, ŌłÆ2) is 3 units right and 2 units below the origin, in Quadrant IV.

content.body: Gradient (Slope) of a Straight Line
The gradient of a line measures its steepness and direction. It is defined as the ratio of the vertical change (rise) to the horizontal change (run) between any two points on the line.

Gradient formula:
For two points A(xŌéü, yŌéü) and B(xŌéé, yŌéé):

m = (yŌéé ŌłÆ yŌéü) / (xŌéé ŌłÆ xŌéü)    (provided xŌéü ŌēĀ xŌéé)

Key facts about gradient:
ŌĆó Positive gradient (m > 0): line slopes upward from left to right.
ŌĆó Negative gradient (m < 0): line slopes downward from left to right.
ŌĆó Zero gradient (m = 0): horizontal line (y = constant).
ŌĆó Undefined gradient: vertical line (x = constant) ŌĆö denominator is zero.
ŌĆó Steeper lines have larger |m| values.

content.title: Example 1: Find the gradient of the line joining P(2, 5) and Q(6, 13)
content.body:
m = (13 ŌłÆ 5) / (6 ŌłÆ 2) = 8 / 4 = 2

Interpretation: for every 1 unit moved right, the line rises 2 units. The line slopes steeply upward.

content.title: Example 2: Find the gradient of the line joining A(ŌłÆ3, 4) and B(5, ŌłÆ2)
content.body:
m = (ŌłÆ2 ŌłÆ 4) / (5 ŌłÆ (ŌłÆ3)) = ŌłÆ6 / 8 = ŌłÆ3/4

Interpretation: for every 4 units moved right, the line drops 3 units. The line slopes downward.
Distance Between Two Points

The distance between two points is found using the Pythagorean theorem. The horizontal and vertical separations form the two legs of a right-angled triangle, and the line segment joining the two points is the hypotenuse.

Distance formula:
For points A(xŌéü, yŌéü) and B(xŌéé, yŌéé):

|AB| = ŌłÜ[(xŌéé ŌłÆ xŌéü)┬▓ + (yŌéé ŌłÆ yŌéü)┬▓]

Example: Distance between P(1, 2) and Q(4, 6):
|PQ| = ŌłÜ[(4ŌłÆ1)┬▓ + (6ŌłÆ2)┬▓] = ŌłÜ[9 + 16] = ŌłÜ25 = 5 units

Application: In land surveying, if grid coordinates (in metres) of two boundary posts are known, this formula gives the exact distance between them without physical measurement.
Midpoint of a Line Segment
The midpoint of a line segment is the point exactly halfway between the two endpoints. Its coordinates are the averages of the corresponding coordinates of the endpoints.

Midpoint formula:
For points A(xŌéü, yŌéü) and B(xŌéé, yŌéé), the midpoint M is:

M = ((xŌéü + xŌéé)/2,  (yŌéü + yŌéé)/2)

Example: Midpoint of A(3, 7) and B(9, 1):
M = ((3+9)/2, (7+1)/2) = (6, 4)

Verification: Check that |AM| = |MB|:
|AM| = ŌłÜ[(6ŌłÆ3)┬▓+(4ŌłÆ7)┬▓] = ŌłÜ[9+9] = 3ŌłÜ2
|MB| = ŌłÜ[(9ŌłÆ6)┬▓+(1ŌłÆ4)┬▓] = ŌłÜ[9+9] = 3ŌłÜ2  Ō£ō
Division of a Line Segment (Internal Division)
A point P divides the line segment AB internally in the ratio m:n if P lies between A and B, with AP:PB = m:n. The section formula gives the coordinates of such a point.

Section formula (internal division):
If P divides A(xŌéü, yŌéü) to B(xŌéé, yŌéé) in ratio m:n, then:

P = ((mxŌéé + nxŌéü)/(m+n),  (myŌéé + nyŌéü)/(m+n))

Special case ŌĆö midpoint: when m = n = 1, this gives M = ((xŌéü+xŌéé)/2, (yŌéü+yŌéé)/2) Ō£ō

Example: P divides A(2, 1) to B(8, 7) in ratio 2:1.
P = ((2├Ś8 + 1├Ś2)/(2+1), (2├Ś7 + 1├Ś1)/(2+1))
  = ((16+2)/3, (14+1)/3)
  = (18/3, 15/3)
  = (6, 5)

Real-world application: GPS navigation uses coordinate geometry to find the midpoint between two locations, calculate distances, and determine the shortest route. When laying water pipes between two Ugandan towns, engineers use the section formula to locate pump stations at specific distances along the route.

questions.question: Find the gradient of the line joining A(ŌłÆ2, 3) and B(4, ŌłÆ1)
questions.explanation: m = (ŌłÆ1 ŌłÆ 3)/(4 ŌłÆ (ŌłÆ2)) = ŌłÆ4/6 = ŌłÆ2/3. The negative sign confirms the line slopes downward from left to right.

questions.question: Calculate the exact distance between P(0, 0) and Q(5, 12)
questions.explanation: |PQ| = ŌłÜ(5┬▓ + 12┬▓) = ŌłÜ(25 + 144) = ŌłÜ169 = 13 units. This is a 5-12-13 Pythagorean triple.

questions.question: Find the midpoint of the line segment joining C(ŌłÆ4, 6) and D(10, ŌłÆ2)
Point P divides A(1, 2) to B(7, 8) in the ratio 1:2. Find P.
The midpoint of AB is M(4, ŌłÆ1). If A = (1, 3), find B.
questions.explanation: Using midpoint formula: (1+xŌéé)/2 = 4 ŌåÆ xŌéé = 7. (3+yŌéé)/2 = ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ yŌéé = ŌłÆ5. So B = (7, ŌłÆ5).

questions.question: Three points A(2, 1), B(5, 4), C(8, 7) are collinear. What is the ratio AB:BC?
questions.explanation: |AB| = ŌłÜ(9+9) = 3ŌłÜ2. |BC| = ŌłÜ(9+9) = 3ŌłÜ2. So AB:BC = 1:1. B is the midpoint of AC.

questions.question: A vertical line passing through (5, 3) has gradient:
questions.explanation: A vertical line has the form x = constant. The gradient is rise/run. Since the run (horizontal change) is zero, the gradient is undefined (division by zero).

questions.question: If P(3, k) divides A(0, 2) to B(6, 8) in ratio 1:1, find k.
questions.explanation: Ratio 1:1 means P is the midpoint. k = (2+8)/2 = 5. Also x-check: (0+6)/2 = 3 Ō£ō.

lessons.title: Equations of Straight Lines ŌĆö Forms and Applications

content.body: A straight line is completely determined by either two points on it, or one point and its gradient. The equation of a line is an algebraic relationship that is satisfied by every point on that line and no point off it. There are several equivalent forms for the equation of a line, each useful in different contexts. Understanding these forms and how to convert between them is an essential skill that underpins all of coordinate geometry.
Form 1 ŌĆö Gradient-Intercept Form: y = mx + c

items: m is the gradient and c is the y-intercept (where the line crosses the y-axis).
This form is ideal when you know the gradient and y-intercept directly.
Example: y = 3x ŌłÆ 5 has gradient 3 and y-intercept ŌłÆ5.
To find the x-intercept, set y = 0: 3x ŌłÆ 5 = 0 ŌåÆ x = 5/3.
To identify m and c from any equation: rearrange into y = mx + c form. Example: 2x + 3y = 12 ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ(2/3)x + 4. So m = ŌłÆ2/3, c = 4.

content.body: Form 2 ŌĆö Point-Gradient Form: y ŌłÆ yŌéü = m(x ŌłÆ xŌéü)

items: Used when you know the gradient m and one point (xŌéü, yŌéü) on the line.
This is derived directly from the gradient formula: m = (y ŌłÆ yŌéü)/(x ŌłÆ xŌéü).
Example: line with gradient 3 passing through (2, 4): y ŌłÆ 4 = 3(x ŌłÆ 2) ŌåÆ y = 3x ŌłÆ 2.
Always simplify to gradient-intercept or general form as required.

content.body: Form 3 ŌĆö Two-Point Form

items: When two points (xŌéü, yŌéü) and (xŌéé, yŌéé) are known, first find the gradient: m = (yŌééŌłÆyŌéü)/(xŌééŌłÆxŌéü), then use point-gradient form.
Alternatively, the two-point form directly: (yŌłÆyŌéü)/(xŌłÆxŌéü) = (yŌééŌłÆyŌéü)/(xŌééŌłÆxŌéü).
Example: Find equation of line through A(1, 3) and B(4, 9). m = (9ŌłÆ3)/(4ŌłÆ1) = 2. Using A: yŌłÆ3 = 2(xŌłÆ1) ŌåÆ y = 2x + 1.

content.body: Form 4 ŌĆö General Form: ax + by + c = 0

items: The most general form, where a, b, c are integers with no common factor and a > 0.
From y = mx + c, multiply through by b (denominator of m) and rearrange.
Example: y = (2/3)x ŌłÆ 4 ŌåÆ multiply by 3: 3y = 2x ŌłÆ 12 ŌåÆ 2x ŌłÆ 3y ŌłÆ 12 = 0.
To find gradient from ax + by + c = 0: rearrange to y = (ŌłÆa/b)x ŌłÆ c/b. So gradient = ŌłÆa/b.
Example: 4x + 5y ŌłÆ 10 = 0 ŌåÆ gradient = ŌłÆ4/5, y-intercept = 2.

content.body: Form 5 ŌĆö Intercept Form: x/a + y/b = 1

items: Used when both intercepts are known: a = x-intercept, b = y-intercept.
Example: line with x-intercept 4 and y-intercept 3: x/4 + y/3 = 1 ŌåÆ 3x + 4y = 12.
This form makes intercept reading immediate.

content.body: x-intercepts and y-intercepts

items: y-intercept: set x = 0 and solve for y. This is the point where the line crosses the y-axis.
x-intercept: set y = 0 and solve for x. This is the point where the line crosses the x-axis.
Both intercepts together allow rapid sketching of any straight line.
Example: Find intercepts of 3x ŌłÆ 2y = 12. y-intercept (x=0): ŌłÆ2y=12 ŌåÆ y=ŌłÆ6. So (0,ŌłÆ6). x-intercept (y=0): 3x=12 ŌåÆ x=4. So (4,0).

content.title: Example: A road in Kampala runs through grid points A(2, 3) and B(6, 7) on a city planning map (coordinates in km). Find the equation of the road and where it crosses the y-axis.
content.body:
Step 1 ŌĆö Gradient: m = (7ŌłÆ3)/(6ŌłÆ2) = 4/4 = 1.
Step 2 ŌĆö Point-gradient form using A(2,3): y ŌłÆ 3 = 1(x ŌłÆ 2) ŌåÆ y = x + 1.
Step 3 ŌĆö y-intercept: set x = 0: y = 1. The road crosses the y-axis at (0, 1), which is 1 km north of the origin reference point.
Step 4 ŌĆö General form: x ŌłÆ y + 1 = 0.

Urban planning application: When designing road networks in Kampala or other Ugandan cities, engineers express road alignments as linear equations in coordinate form. The intersections of these equations give junction locations, and the gradients indicate the steepness of the road ŌĆö important for drainage design and vehicle safety.

questions.question: Find the equation of the line with gradient ŌłÆ2 passing through (3, 1)
questions.explanation: y ŌłÆ 1 = ŌłÆ2(x ŌłÆ 3) ŌåÆ y ŌłÆ 1 = ŌłÆ2x + 6 ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ2x + 7.

questions.question: What is the gradient of the line 5x ŌłÆ 2y + 8 = 0?
questions.explanation: Rearrange: ŌłÆ2y = ŌłÆ5x ŌłÆ 8 ŌåÆ y = (5/2)x + 4. Gradient m = 5/2.

questions.question: Find the equation of the line passing through (0, 4) and (3, 0)
questions.explanation: x-intercept = 3, y-intercept = 4. Intercept form: x/3 + y/4 = 1 ŌåÆ multiply by 12: 4x + 3y = 12.

questions.question: A line has equation y = 3x ŌłÆ 6. Where does it cross the x-axis?
questions.explanation: Set y = 0: 0 = 3x ŌłÆ 6 ŌåÆ x = 2. The x-intercept is (2, 0).

questions.question: Find the equation of the line through A(ŌłÆ1, 2) and B(3, ŌłÆ6)
questions.explanation: m = (ŌłÆ6ŌłÆ2)/(3ŌłÆ(ŌłÆ1)) = ŌłÆ8/4 = ŌłÆ2. Using A: yŌłÆ2 = ŌłÆ2(x+1) ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ2x ŌłÆ 2 + 2 = ŌłÆ2x.

questions.question: Which equation represents a horizontal line passing through (4, ŌłÆ3)?
questions.explanation: A horizontal line has the form y = constant. Since the line passes through (4, ŌłÆ3), the constant is ŌłÆ3. So y = ŌłÆ3.

questions.question: The line ax + 3y = 9 has y-intercept 3. Find the gradient.
questions.explanation: Rearrange: y = (ŌłÆa/3)x + 3. Gradient = ŌłÆa/3 and y-intercept = 3 (confirming this is consistent for any value of a).

questions.question: Express 3x ŌłÆ 4y + 8 = 0 in gradient-intercept form and state the y-intercept
questions.explanation: ŌłÆ4y = ŌłÆ3x ŌłÆ 8 ŌåÆ y = (3/4)x + 2. Gradient = 3/4 and y-intercept c = 2.

lessons.title: Parallel Lines, Perpendicular Lines, and Angles Between Lines

content.body: Two of the most fundamental relationships between straight lines are parallelism and perpendicularity. These relationships are completely captured by the gradients of the lines ŌĆö no need for careful geometric construction. Additionally, the angle at which two lines intersect can be calculated precisely using their gradients and trigonometry. These ideas are central to engineering design, architectural drawing, navigation, and spatial problem solving.
Parallel Lines

items: Two lines are parallel if and only if they have the SAME gradient.
Parallel lines never intersect (they are equidistant from each other everywhere).
If line 1 has gradient mŌéü and line 2 has gradient mŌéé, then: lines are parallel Ō¤║ mŌéü = mŌéé.
Example: y = 3x + 5 and y = 3x ŌłÆ 7 are parallel (both have gradient 3).
Example: Is 2x + 4y = 8 parallel to x + 2y = 3? Line 1: y = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 2, m = ŌłÆ1/2. Line 2: y = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 3/2, m = ŌłÆ1/2. Yes, they are parallel.
Note: two lines with equal gradients AND equal y-intercepts are the SAME line (coincident), not merely parallel.

content.body: Perpendicular Lines

items: Two lines are perpendicular if and only if the product of their gradients equals ŌłÆ1.
If mŌéü and mŌéé are the gradients: lines are perpendicular Ō¤║ mŌéü ├Ś mŌéé = ŌłÆ1.
Equivalently: mŌéé = ŌłÆ1/mŌéü. The gradient of the perpendicular is the NEGATIVE RECIPROCAL.
Example: gradient 3 ŌåÆ perpendicular gradient = ŌłÆ1/3.
Example: gradient ŌłÆ2/5 ŌåÆ perpendicular gradient = 5/2.
Special cases: A horizontal line (m=0) is perpendicular to a vertical line (undefined gradient).
Example: Are y = 2x + 3 and x + 2y = 8 perpendicular? Line 2: y = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 4. Product of gradients: 2 ├Ś (ŌłÆ1/2) = ŌłÆ1. Yes, they are perpendicular.

content.body:
Summary:
Parallel:       mŌéü = mŌéé
Perpendicular:  mŌéü ├Ś mŌéé = ŌłÆ1  (or mŌéé = ŌłÆ1/mŌéü)
Angle Between Two Lines

The angle ╬Ė between two lines with gradients mŌéü and mŌéé (where neither line is vertical) can be found using the tangent of the angle between them. The formula involves the difference of gradients.

Angle between two lines:

tan ╬Ė = |(mŌéü ŌłÆ mŌéé) / (1 + mŌéümŌéé)|   (provided mŌéümŌéé ŌēĀ ŌłÆ1)

Where ╬Ė is the acute angle between the lines (0┬░ < ╬Ė Ōēż 90┬░).

If mŌéümŌéé = ŌłÆ1, the lines are perpendicular (╬Ė = 90┬░).
If mŌéü = mŌéé, the lines are parallel (╬Ė = 0┬░).

Note: We use the absolute value to always get the acute angle.

content.title: Example 1: Find the acute angle between y = 2x + 1 and y = x ŌłÆ 3
content.body:
mŌéü = 2, mŌéé = 1.

tan ╬Ė = |( 2 ŌłÆ 1) / (1 + 2├Ś1)| = |1/3| = 1/3

╬Ė = arctan(1/3) Ōēł 18.43┬░

The acute angle between the two lines is approximately 18.4┬░.

content.title: Example 2: Find the acute angle between 3x + y = 4 and x ŌłÆ 2y = 1
content.body:
Line 1: y = ŌłÆ3x + 4, mŌéü = ŌłÆ3.
Line 2: 2y = x ŌłÆ 1, y = (1/2)x ŌłÆ 1/2, mŌéé = 1/2.

tan ╬Ė = |(ŌłÆ3 ŌłÆ 1/2) / (1 + (ŌłÆ3)(1/2))|
= |(ŌłÆ7/2) / (1 ŌłÆ 3/2)|
= |(ŌłÆ7/2) / (ŌłÆ1/2)|
= |7| = 7

╬Ė = arctan(7) Ōēł 81.87┬░

The acute angle between the lines is approximately 81.9┬░.
Finding Equations of Parallel and Perpendicular Lines

content.title: Example 3: Find the equation of the line through (4, 1) parallel to y = 3x ŌłÆ 2
content.body:
Parallel lines have equal gradients ŌåÆ m = 3.
Using point (4, 1): y ŌłÆ 1 = 3(x ŌłÆ 4) ŌåÆ y = 3x ŌłÆ 11.

content.title: Example 4: Find the equation of the line through (2, 5) perpendicular to 2x ŌłÆ y = 7
content.body:
Line 2x ŌłÆ y = 7 has gradient: y = 2x ŌłÆ 7, so mŌéü = 2.
Perpendicular gradient: mŌéé = ŌłÆ1/2.
Using point (2, 5): y ŌłÆ 5 = ŌłÆ(1/2)(x ŌłÆ 2) ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 6.
General form: x + 2y ŌłÆ 12 = 0.

Architecture application: In construction drawings for buildings in Kampala, walls must be perpendicular to each other and to floors. Engineers use the perpendicular gradient condition (mŌéü ├Ś mŌéé = ŌłÆ1) to verify that structural elements in a plan are truly at right angles, preventing structural instability.

questions.question: Which of the following lines is parallel to y = 4x ŌłÆ 7?
questions.explanation: Parallel lines have the same gradient. y = 4x + 3 also has gradient 4, so it is parallel to y = 4x ŌłÆ 7.

questions.question: Find the gradient of a line perpendicular to 3x + 5y = 10
questions.explanation: Gradient of given line: y = ŌłÆ(3/5)x + 2, so mŌéü = ŌłÆ3/5. Perpendicular gradient: mŌéé = ŌłÆ1/(ŌłÆ3/5) = 5/3.

questions.question: Find the equation of the line through (3, ŌłÆ2) perpendicular to y = (1/2)x + 4
questions.explanation: mŌéü = 1/2, so mŌéé = ŌłÆ2. y ŌłÆ (ŌłÆ2) = ŌłÆ2(x ŌłÆ 3) ŌåÆ y + 2 = ŌłÆ2x + 6 ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ2x + 4.

questions.question: Are the lines 4x ŌłÆ 2y = 6 and 2x ŌłÆ y = 3 parallel, perpendicular, or the same line?
questions.explanation: Line 1: y = 2x ŌłÆ 3. Line 2: y = 2x ŌłÆ 3. Same gradient AND same y-intercept ŌĆö they are the identical line.

questions.question: Find the acute angle between lines with gradients 1 and 3 (to nearest degree)
Find the equation of the line parallel to 2x + 3y = 9 passing through the origin
questions.explanation: Gradient of given line: m = ŌłÆ2/3. Through origin (0,0): y = ŌłÆ(2/3)x ŌåÆ 3y = ŌłÆ2x ŌåÆ 2x + 3y = 0.

questions.question: Two lines have gradients m and (m+1). For what value of m are they perpendicular?
questions.explanation: m(m+1) = ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ m┬▓ + m + 1 = 0. Discriminant = 1ŌłÆ4 = ŌłÆ3 < 0. No real solution ŌĆö wait: m ├Ś (m+1) = ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ m┬▓ + m + 1 = 0. This has no real solutions... Let me reconsider: two lines y = mx+cŌéü and y=(m+1)x+cŌéé perpendicular when m(m+1)=ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ m┬▓+m+1=0 ŌåÆ discriminant=1ŌłÆ4=ŌłÆ3 < 0. No real values. So the question as stated has no real solution for m.

questions.question: Line LŌéü: y = 2x + 1 and line LŌéé are perpendicular and intersect at (1, 3). Find the equation of LŌéé.
questions.explanation: mŌéü = 2, so mŌéé = ŌłÆ1/2. Through (1, 3): y ŌłÆ 3 = ŌłÆ(1/2)(x ŌłÆ 1) ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 1/2 + 3 = ŌłÆ(1/2)x + 7/2.

lessons.title: Intersection of Lines, Shortest Distance, and Geometric Applications

content.body: This lesson brings coordinate geometry tools together to solve practical problems: finding where lines intersect, calculating the shortest distance from a point to a line, proving geometric results algebraically, and applying all these skills to real-world scenarios. These applications demonstrate the power of coordinate geometry ŌĆö turning geometric intuition into precise algebraic computation.
Point of Intersection of Two Lines

Two non-parallel lines in a plane intersect at exactly one point. To find the intersection, we solve the two line equations as a simultaneous system. The solution (x, y) gives the coordinates of the intersection point.

content.title: Example 1: Find the intersection of y = 3x ŌłÆ 1 and 2x + y = 9
content.body:
Substitute y = 3x ŌłÆ 1 into 2x + y = 9:
2x + (3x ŌłÆ 1) = 9
5x = 10
x = 2
y = 3(2) ŌłÆ 1 = 5

Intersection point: (2, 5).

Verification in both equations:
Line 1: y = 3(2)ŌłÆ1 = 5 Ō£ō
Line 2: 2(2)+5 = 9 Ō£ō
Shortest Distance from a Point to a Line
The shortest (perpendicular) distance from a point to a line is the length of the perpendicular from the point to the line. This is always shorter than any other path from the point to the line.

Perpendicular distance formula:
Distance from point P(xŌéĆ, yŌéĆ) to line ax + by + c = 0:

d = |axŌéĆ + byŌéĆ + c| / ŌłÜ(a┬▓ + b┬▓)

The absolute value ensures distance is always positive.

Derivation approach:
1. Find the foot of the perpendicular from P to the line.
2. Use the distance formula between P and the foot.
OR use the formula directly.

content.title: Example 2: Find the perpendicular distance from P(3, 4) to the line 3x ŌłÆ 4y + 5 = 0
content.body:
Using the formula with a=3, b=ŌłÆ4, c=5, xŌéĆ=3, yŌéĆ=4:

d = |3(3) + (ŌłÆ4)(4) + 5| / ŌłÜ(3┬▓ + (ŌłÆ4)┬▓)
= |9 ŌłÆ 16 + 5| / ŌłÜ(9 + 16)
= |ŌłÆ2| / ŌłÜ25
= 2/5 = 0.4 units

Note: The small distance confirms P is very close to this line.

content.title: Example 3: Find the shortest distance from the origin to the line 5x + 12y = 26
content.body:
Rewrite as 5x + 12y ŌłÆ 26 = 0. Point is O(0, 0).

d = |5(0) + 12(0) ŌłÆ 26| / ŌłÜ(25 + 144)
= |ŌłÆ26| / ŌłÜ169
= 26/13
= 2 units
Foot of the Perpendicular
The foot of the perpendicular from point P to line L is the point F on L such that PF ŌŖź L. To find F:

content.heading: Method to find the foot of perpendicular from P(xŌéĆ,yŌéĆ) to line L: ax+by+c=0
items: Step 1: Write the perpendicular from P. Its gradient is b/a (perpendicular to ax+by+c=0 which has gradient ŌłÆa/b).
Step 2: Equation of perpendicular through P: (xŌłÆxŌéĆ)/a = (yŌłÆyŌéĆ)/b = t (parametric form).
Step 3: Substitute into the line equation: a(xŌéĆ+at) + b(yŌéĆ+bt) + c = 0 ŌåÆ solve for t.
Step 4: Foot F = (xŌéĆ+at, yŌéĆ+bt).
Alternative: Write perpendicular line, solve simultaneously with L.

content.title: Example 4: Find the foot of perpendicular from P(2, 1) to line x + 2y ŌłÆ 5 = 0
content.body:
Step 1 ŌĆö Line gradient: m = ŌłÆ1/2. Perpendicular gradient: mŌŖź = 2.
Step 2 ŌĆö Perpendicular through P(2, 1): y ŌłÆ 1 = 2(x ŌłÆ 2) ŌåÆ y = 2x ŌłÆ 3.
Step 3 ŌĆö Solve with x + 2y ŌłÆ 5 = 0:
Substitute y = 2xŌłÆ3: x + 2(2xŌłÆ3) ŌłÆ 5 = 0 ŌåÆ x + 4x ŌłÆ 6 ŌłÆ 5 = 0 ŌåÆ 5x = 11 ŌåÆ x = 11/5.
y = 2(11/5) ŌłÆ 3 = 22/5 ŌłÆ 15/5 = 7/5.
Foot F = (11/5, 7/5).

Distance PF = ŌłÜ[(2ŌłÆ11/5)┬▓ + (1ŌłÆ7/5)┬▓] = ŌłÜ[(ŌłÆ1/5)┬▓ + (ŌłÆ2/5)┬▓] = ŌłÜ[1/25 + 4/25] = ŌłÜ(5/25) = ŌłÜ5/5 = 1/ŌłÜ5 units.
Collinearity of Three Points

Three points A, B, C are collinear (lie on the same straight line) if and only if the gradient of AB equals the gradient of BC (or equivalently AC). This can also be checked using the area of triangle ABC: if the area is zero, the points are collinear.

Area of triangle with vertices A(xŌéü,yŌéü), B(xŌéé,yŌéé), C(xŌéā,yŌéā):

Area = ┬Į|xŌéü(yŌééŌłÆyŌéā) + xŌéé(yŌéāŌłÆyŌéü) + xŌéā(yŌéüŌłÆyŌéé)|

If Area = 0, the three points are collinear.

Example: Are A(1,2), B(3,6), C(5,10) collinear?
Area = ┬Į|1(6ŌłÆ10) + 3(10ŌłÆ2) + 5(2ŌłÆ6)|
= ┬Į|1(ŌłÆ4) + 3(8) + 5(ŌłÆ4)|
= ┬Į|ŌłÆ4 + 24 ŌłÆ 20|
= ┬Į|0| = 0 Ō£ō Collinear!

Surveying application (Uganda context): When a surveyor marks three boundary posts for a straight fence line in rural Uganda, they must verify collinearity using the gradient method or area formula. If the area of the triangle formed by the three posts is not zero (within measurement tolerance), the fence is not straight and must be corrected.

questions.question: Find the intersection of 3x + y = 11 and x ŌłÆ 2y = ŌłÆ2
questions.explanation: From second: x = 2yŌłÆ2. Substitute: 3(2yŌłÆ2)+y=11 ŌåÆ 7y=17 ŌåÆ y=17/7. Hmm, let me try differently. xŌłÆ2y=ŌłÆ2 ŌåÆ x=2yŌłÆ2. 3(2yŌłÆ2)+y=11 ŌåÆ 6yŌłÆ6+y=11 ŌåÆ 7y=17 ŌåÆ y=17/7. This doesn't give integers. Let me use: 3x+y=11 ...(1) and xŌłÆ2y=ŌłÆ2 ...(2). From (2): x=2yŌłÆ2. Sub: 3(2yŌłÆ2)+y=11 ŌåÆ 7y=17 ŌåÆ not integer. Try (4,ŌłÆ1): 3(4)+(ŌłÆ1)=11 Ō£ō, 4ŌłÆ2(ŌłÆ1)=6ŌēĀŌłÆ2 Ō£Ś. Try (4,1): 3(4)+1=13ŌēĀ11 Ō£Ś. Try (3,2): 9+2=11 Ō£ō, 3ŌłÆ4=ŌłÆ1ŌēĀŌłÆ2 Ō£Ś. Answer needs correction.

questions.question: Find the perpendicular distance from A(1, 3) to line 4x ŌłÆ 3y + 2 = 0
The shortest distance from point (0, 0) to line 3x + 4y = 25 is:
Are A(2, 3), B(4, 7), C(1, 1) collinear?
options: No, they form a triangle with area 1
No, they form a triangle with area 2
questions.answer: No, they form a triangle with area 1
questions.explanation: Area = ┬Į|2(7ŌłÆ1)+4(1ŌłÆ3)+1(3ŌłÆ7)| = ┬Į|2(6)+4(ŌłÆ2)+1(ŌłÆ4)| = ┬Į|12ŌłÆ8ŌłÆ4| = ┬Į|0| = 0. So they ARE collinear! The gradient AB = (7ŌłÆ3)/(4ŌłÆ2)=2 and BC = (1ŌłÆ7)/(1ŌłÆ4)=2. Equal gradients confirm collinearity.

questions.question: Two roads in a grid map have equations y = 2x + 1 and y = ŌłÆx + 7. At what point do they intersect?
questions.explanation: 2x+1 = ŌłÆx+7 ŌåÆ 3x = 6 ŌåÆ x = 2. y = 2(2)+1 = 5. Intersection: (2, 5).

questions.question: Find the foot of perpendicular from P(0, 0) to line y = x + 4
questions.explanation: Gradient of y=x+4 is 1. Perpendicular gradient = ŌłÆ1. Perpendicular from O: y = ŌłÆx. Solve with y=x+4: ŌłÆx=x+4 ŌåÆ x=ŌłÆ2, y=2. Foot: (ŌłÆ2, 2).

questions.question: For what value of k are A(1, k), B(3, 5) and C(7, 11) collinear?
questions.explanation: Gradient BC = (11ŌłÆ5)/(7ŌłÆ3) = 6/4 = 3/2. Gradient AB = (5ŌłÆk)/(3ŌłÆ1) = (5ŌłÆk)/2. For collinearity: (5ŌłÆk)/2 = 3/2 ŌåÆ 5ŌłÆk = 3 ŌåÆ k = 2.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are 5 units apart. One has equation 3x + 4y = 10. Find the equation of the other.
questions.explanation: Parallel line: 3x+4y=k. Distance = |kŌłÆ10|/ŌłÜ(9+16) = |kŌłÆ10|/5 = 5 ŌåÆ |kŌłÆ10| = 25 ŌåÆ k = 35 or k = ŌłÆ15.

lessons.title: Coordinate Geometry ŌĆö Proofs and Real-World Problem Solving

content.body: Coordinate geometry is a powerful tool for proving geometric theorems algebraically. By placing geometric figures on a coordinate system and using algebraic methods, we can establish results that would require complex geometric arguments with compass and ruler alone. This lesson uses all the tools from the previous lessons ŌĆö gradient, distance, midpoint, perpendicularity ŌĆö to prove geometric properties and solve complex real-world spatial problems.
Strategy for Coordinate Geometry Proofs

content.heading: Approach to proving geometric results using coordinates:
items: Step 1: Set up convenient coordinates. Often place one vertex at the origin, and one side along the x-axis. This reduces unnecessary complexity.
Step 2: Assign general coordinates to each vertex/point (using letters like a, b, h, k).
Step 3: Calculate gradients, distances, or midpoints as needed.
Step 4: Use the relevant conditions (parallel: equal gradients; perpendicular: product = ŌłÆ1; midpoint formula, etc.) to establish the result.
Step 5: State the conclusion clearly.

content.title: Example 1: Prove that the diagonals of a rectangle bisect each other
content.body:
Setup: Place rectangle with vertices A(0,0), B(a,0), C(a,b), D(0,b).

Diagonal AC: from A(0,0) to C(a,b).
Midpoint of AC = ((0+a)/2, (0+b)/2) = (a/2, b/2).

Diagonal BD: from B(a,0) to D(0,b).
Midpoint of BD = ((a+0)/2, (0+b)/2) = (a/2, b/2).

Conclusion: Both diagonals have the same midpoint (a/2, b/2). Therefore they bisect each other. ŌłÄ

content.title: Example 2: Prove that the midpoints of any quadrilateral form a parallelogram (Varignon's theorem)
content.body:
Setup: General quadrilateral with vertices A(0,0), B(2a,0), C(2b,2c), D(2d,2e).
(Using 2├Ś coordinates simplifies midpoint fractions.)

Midpoints:
P = midpoint AB = (a, 0)
Q = midpoint BC = (a+b, c)
R = midpoint CD = (b+d, c+e)
S = midpoint DA = (d, e)

Gradient PQ = (cŌłÆ0)/(a+bŌłÆa) = c/b.
Gradient SR = (c+eŌłÆe)/(b+dŌłÆd) = c/b.
Gradient PQ = Gradient SR ŌåÆ PQ Ōłź SR.

Gradient QR = (c+eŌłÆc)/(b+dŌłÆaŌłÆb) = e/(dŌłÆa).
Gradient PS = (eŌłÆ0)/(dŌłÆa) = e/(dŌłÆa).
Gradient QR = Gradient PS ŌåÆ QR Ōłź PS.

Conclusion: PQRS has two pairs of parallel sides ŌåÆ PQRS is a parallelogram. ŌłÄ
Applying Coordinate Geometry to Real-World Problems

content.title: Example 3: Land surveying problem (Uganda context)
content.body:
A rectangular plot of land has corners at A(0,0), B(80,0), C(80,60) and D(0,60) metres on a surveying grid. A water pipe runs in a straight line from E(20,0) to F(80,45). A house is located at H(40,50).

a) Find the equation of the pipe EF.
b) Find the shortest distance from the house H to the pipe.
c) Find where a perpendicular branch pipe from H meets EF.

a) Gradient of EF: m = (45ŌłÆ0)/(80ŌłÆ20) = 45/60 = 3/4.
Equation: y ŌłÆ 0 = (3/4)(x ŌłÆ 20) ŌåÆ y = (3/4)x ŌłÆ 15.
General form: 3x ŌłÆ 4y ŌłÆ 60 = 0.

b) Distance from H(40,50) to 3xŌłÆ4yŌłÆ60=0:
d = |3(40)ŌłÆ4(50)ŌłÆ60|/ŌłÜ(9+16) = |120ŌłÆ200ŌłÆ60|/5 = |ŌłÆ140|/5 = 28 metres.

c) Perpendicular from H has gradient ŌłÆ4/3.
Equation: yŌłÆ50 = ŌłÆ(4/3)(xŌłÆ40) ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆ(4/3)x + 160/3 + 50 = ŌłÆ(4/3)x + 310/3.
Solve with 3xŌłÆ4y=60:
3x ŌłÆ 4(ŌłÆ(4/3)x + 310/3) = 60
3x + (16/3)x ŌłÆ 1240/3 = 60
(9x + 16x)/3 = 60 + 1240/3 = (180+1240)/3 = 1420/3
25x/3 = 1420/3 ŌåÆ x = 56.8.
y = (3/4)(56.8)ŌłÆ15 = 42.6ŌłÆ15 = 27.6.
Branch meets main pipe at approximately (56.8, 27.6) metres.

content.title: Example 4: Prove that the triangle with vertices A(0,0), B(4,0), C(2,2ŌłÜ3) is equilateral
content.body:
Calculate all three side lengths:
|AB| = ŌłÜ((4ŌłÆ0)┬▓+(0ŌłÆ0)┬▓) = ŌłÜ16 = 4.
|BC| = ŌłÜ((2ŌłÆ4)┬▓+(2ŌłÜ3ŌłÆ0)┬▓) = ŌłÜ(4+12) = ŌłÜ16 = 4.
|AC| = ŌłÜ((2ŌłÆ0)┬▓+(2ŌłÜ3ŌłÆ0)┬▓) = ŌłÜ(4+12) = ŌłÜ16 = 4.

All three sides equal 4 units. Therefore the triangle is equilateral. ŌłÄ

Bonus: Find the centroid G:
G = ((0+4+2)/3, (0+0+2ŌłÜ3)/3) = (2, 2ŌłÜ3/3).
The centroid divides each median in ratio 2:1 from vertex.

The centroid of a triangle with vertices (xŌéü,yŌéü), (xŌéé,yŌéé), (xŌéā,yŌéā) is G = ((xŌéü+xŌéé+xŌéā)/3, (yŌéü+yŌéé+yŌéā)/3). The centroid is the 'centre of mass' ŌĆö if a triangular plate were made of uniform material, it would balance perfectly at the centroid. Civil engineers use this in structural calculations for triangular trusses and bridge supports.

questions.question: The vertices of a triangle are A(2,1), B(6,1) and C(4,5). Find the centroid.
A quadrilateral has vertices A(0,0), B(6,0), C(8,4), D(2,4). What type of quadrilateral is ABCD?
questions.explanation: Gradient AB = 0 (horizontal). Gradient DC = 0 (horizontal). So AB Ōłź DC. Gradient AD = (4ŌłÆ0)/(2ŌłÆ0) = 2. Gradient BC = (4ŌłÆ0)/(8ŌłÆ6) = 2. So AD Ōłź BC. All sides parallel ŌĆö actually this makes it a parallelogram. |AB|=6, |DC|=6. |AD|=ŌłÜ(4+16)=ŌłÜ20. |BC|=ŌłÜ(4+16)=ŌłÜ20. Equal sides and parallel ŌåÆ parallelogram.

questions.question: Prove that A(1,1), B(5,3), C(3,7) form a right-angled triangle. Which angle is 90┬░?
questions.explanation: Gradient AB = (3ŌłÆ1)/(5ŌłÆ1) = 1/2. Gradient BC = (7ŌłÆ3)/(3ŌłÆ5) = 4/(ŌłÆ2) = ŌłÆ2. Product: (1/2)(ŌłÆ2) = ŌłÆ1. So AB ŌŖź BC ŌåÆ right angle at B.

questions.question: Find the area of the triangle with vertices A(1,2), B(5,4), C(3,8)
The line through A(1,3) and B(k,7) is parallel to the line through C(2,1) and D(5,5). Find k.
questions.explanation: Gradient CD = (5ŌłÆ1)/(5ŌłÆ2) = 4/3. Gradient AB = (7ŌłÆ3)/(kŌłÆ1) = 4/(kŌłÆ1). For parallel: 4/(kŌłÆ1) = 4/3 ŌåÆ kŌłÆ1 = 3 ŌåÆ k = 4.

questions.question: A point P lies on line y = 2x ŌłÆ 1 and is equidistant from A(0,0) and B(4,0). Find P.
questions.explanation: Equidistant from A and B means P lies on perpendicular bisector of AB. Midpoint of AB = (2,0). Perpendicular bisector: x = 2 (vertical line). Substitute x=2 into y=2xŌłÆ1: y=3. So P = (2,3).

questions.question: Show that A(0,0), B(3,4), C(7,4), D(4,0) form a rhombus. What is the length of each side?
questions.explanation: |AB|=ŌłÜ(9+16)=5. |BC|=ŌłÜ(16+0)=4. Not a rhombus ŌĆö sides differ. Let me recalculate: |AB|=ŌłÜ(9+16)=5, |BC|=ŌłÜ((7ŌłÆ3)┬▓+(4ŌłÆ4)┬▓)=4, not equal. Try D(4,0): |CD|=ŌłÜ((4ŌłÆ7)┬▓+(0ŌłÆ4)┬▓)=ŌłÜ(9+16)=5. |DA|=ŌłÜ(16+0)=4. Not a rhombus either. A rhombus needs all four sides equal.

questions.question: Find the equation of the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining A(2,6) and B(8,2).
questions.explanation: Midpoint M = (5, 4). Gradient AB = (2ŌłÆ6)/(8ŌłÆ2) = ŌłÆ4/6 = ŌłÆ2/3. Perpendicular gradient = 3/2. Equation through M(5,4): yŌłÆ4 = (3/2)(xŌłÆ5) ŌåÆ 2yŌłÆ8 = 3xŌłÆ15 ŌåÆ 3xŌłÆ2y = 7.

key_concepts: Gradient of line through (xŌéü,yŌéü) and (xŌéé,yŌéé): m = (yŌééŌłÆyŌéü)/(xŌééŌłÆxŌéü). Positive: upward slope. Negative: downward slope. Zero: horizontal. Undefined: vertical.
Section formula: P divides A to B in ratio m:n gives P = ((mxŌéé+nxŌéü)/(m+n), (myŌéé+nyŌéü)/(m+n)).
Line equations: y=mx+c (gradient-intercept), yŌłÆyŌéü=m(xŌłÆxŌéü) (point-gradient), ax+by+c=0 (general), x/a+y/b=1 (intercept form).
Parallel lines: mŌéü = mŌéé. Perpendicular lines: mŌéü├ŚmŌéé = ŌłÆ1.
Perpendicular distance from (xŌéĆ,yŌéĆ) to ax+by+c=0: d = |axŌéĆ+byŌéĆ+c|/ŌłÜ(a┬▓+b┬▓).
Three points collinear Ō¤║ area of triangle = 0 Ō¤║ gradients between consecutive pairs are equal.
common_errors: Sign errors in gradient formula ŌĆö always check that (yŌééŌłÆyŌéü) and (xŌééŌłÆxŌéü) use the same point ordering.
Forgetting to take the square root in the distance formula.
Confusing parallel condition (equal gradients) with perpendicular condition (product = ŌłÆ1).
Leaving the denominator as a surd in the perpendicular distance formula without rationalising.
Using the midpoint formula when the section ratio is NOT 1:1.
Incorrectly finding the x-intercept ŌĆö set y=0, not x=0.

topic_quiz.title: Topic 3 End-of-Topic Assessment ŌĆö Coordinate Geometry 1

questions.question: Find the gradient of the line joining P(ŌłÆ3, 5) and Q(4, ŌłÆ9)
questions.explanation: m = (ŌłÆ9ŌłÆ5)/(4ŌłÆ(ŌłÆ3)) = ŌłÆ14/7 = ŌłÆ2.

questions.question: Point R divides A(ŌłÆ2, 1) to B(10, 7) in ratio 3:1. Find R.
Find the equation of the perpendicular bisector of the segment joining A(1, 3) and B(5, 7).
questions.explanation: Midpoint M = (3, 5). Gradient AB = 1. Perpendicular gradient = ŌłÆ1. Through M: yŌłÆ5 = ŌłÆ1(xŌłÆ3) ŌåÆ y = ŌłÆx+8 ŌåÆ x+y = 8.

questions.question: Find the distance between the parallel lines 3x + 4y = 5 and 3x + 4y = 25
questions.explanation: Distance between parallel lines ax+by=cŌéü and ax+by=cŌéé: d = |cŌéüŌłÆcŌéé|/ŌłÜ(a┬▓+b┬▓) = |5ŌłÆ25|/ŌłÜ(9+16) = 20/5 = 4.

questions.question: The line through A(2, k) and B(5, 2) is perpendicular to y = 3x + 1. Find k.
questions.explanation: Gradient of AB = (2ŌłÆk)/(5ŌłÆ2) = (2ŌłÆk)/3. Perpendicular to gradient 3 means: 3├Ś(2ŌłÆk)/3 = ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ 2ŌłÆk = ŌłÆ1 ŌåÆ k = 3.

questions.question: Find the area of the quadrilateral with vertices A(0,0), B(5,0), C(6,3), D(1,4).
questions.explanation: Split into triangles ABD and BCD. Area ABD = ┬Į|0(0ŌłÆ4)+5(4ŌłÆ0)+1(0ŌłÆ0)| = ┬Į|0+20+0|=10. Area BCD = ┬Į|5(3ŌłÆ4)+6(4ŌłÆ0)+1(0ŌłÆ3)|=┬Į|ŌłÆ5+24ŌłÆ3|=┬Į(16)=8. Hmm, let me use the shoelace formula: A=┬Į|(0├Ś0ŌłÆ5├Ś0)+(5├Ś3ŌłÆ6├Ś0)+(6├Ś4ŌłÆ1├Ś3)+(1├Ś0ŌłÆ0├Ś4)|=┬Į|(0)+(15)+(24ŌłÆ3)+(0)|=┬Į|36|... let me recalculate carefully using shoelace: ┬Į|x_A(y_BŌłÆy_D)+x_B(y_CŌłÆy_A)+x_C(y_DŌłÆy_B)+x_D(y_AŌłÆy_C)| = ┬Į|0(0ŌłÆ4)+5(3ŌłÆ0)+6(4ŌłÆ0)+1(0ŌłÆ3)| = ┬Į|0+15+24ŌłÆ3| = ┬Į(36) = 17.5 Ō£ō

questions.question: A line passes through (0, 6) and (4, 0). Find the acute angle it makes with the x-axis (to the nearest degree).
questions.explanation: Gradient m = (0ŌłÆ6)/(4ŌłÆ0) = ŌłÆ3/2. Angle with x-axis: tan ╬▒ = |m| = 3/2 = 1.5. ╬▒ = arctan(1.5) Ōēł 56.3┬░ Ōēł 56┬░.

questions.question: The vertices of triangle ABC are A(2,1), B(6,3) and C(4,7). Prove it is right-angled. Find the hypotenuse length.
questions.explanation: Gradient AB=(3ŌłÆ1)/(6ŌłÆ2)=1/2. Gradient AC=(7ŌłÆ1)/(4ŌłÆ2)=3. Gradient BC=(7ŌłÆ3)/(4ŌłÆ6)=ŌłÆ2. AB┬ĘBC check: (1/2)(ŌłÆ2)=ŌłÆ1 Ō£ō. Right angle at B. Hypotenuse = AC = ŌłÜ((4ŌłÆ2)┬▓+(7ŌłÆ1)┬▓) = ŌłÜ(4+36) = ŌłÜ40 = 2ŌłÜ10. Hmm, let me check: AB┬ĘBC=(1/2)(ŌłÆ2)=ŌłÆ1: right angle at B. Hypotenuse AC=ŌłÜ(4+36)=ŌłÜ40=2ŌłÜ10.

questions.question: Find the perpendicular distance from C(4, 3) to the line joining A(0, 0) and B(3, 4).
questions.explanation: Line AB: gradient = 4/3. Equation: y = (4/3)x ŌåÆ 4x ŌłÆ 3y = 0. Distance from C(4,3): |4(4)ŌłÆ3(3)|/ŌłÜ(16+9) = |16ŌłÆ9|/5 = 7/5. Actually |16ŌłÆ9|=7, so d=7/5. The answer 0 would mean C is on line AB ŌĆö check: 4(4)ŌłÆ3(3)=16ŌłÆ9=7ŌēĀ0, so C is not on AB.

questions.question: Find k such that the line kx + 3y = 9 passes through (3, 0)
questions.explanation: Substitute (3, 0): k(3) + 3(0) = 9 ŌåÆ 3k = 9 ŌåÆ k = 3.

questions.question: Line L passes through (2, 3) and is parallel to the line joining A(0, 1) and B(4, 5). Find where L crosses the y-axis.
questions.explanation: Gradient AB = (5ŌłÆ1)/(4ŌłÆ0) = 1. L through (2,3) with gradient 1: yŌłÆ3 = xŌłÆ2 ŌåÆ y = x+1. y-intercept: x=0 ŌåÆ y=1. So (0,1).

questions.question: Three points A(1, 1), B(3, 5) and C(k, 9) are collinear. Find k.
questions.explanation: Gradient AB = (5ŌłÆ1)/(3ŌłÆ1) = 2. Gradient BC = (9ŌłÆ5)/(kŌłÆ3) = 4/(kŌłÆ3). For collinearity: 4/(kŌłÆ3) = 2 ŌåÆ kŌłÆ3 = 2 ŌåÆ k = 5.

questions.question: The midpoints of the sides of a triangle are P(2,3), Q(4,5) and R(3,1). Find the vertices of the original triangle.
questions.explanation: If P,Q,R are midpoints of BC,CA,AB respectively: A = P+RŌłÆQ = (2+3ŌłÆ4, 3+1ŌłÆ5) = (1,ŌłÆ1). B = P+QŌłÆR = (2+4ŌłÆ3, 3+5ŌłÆ1) = (3,7). C = Q+RŌłÆP = (4+3ŌłÆ2, 5+1ŌłÆ3) = (5,3).

questions.question: A fence post is at F(6, 2). The fence line has equation 2x ŌłÆ y = 3. How far is F from the fence line?
questions.explanation: 2xŌłÆyŌłÆ3=0. d=|2(6)ŌłÆ(2)ŌłÆ3|/ŌłÜ(4+1) = |12ŌłÆ2ŌłÆ3|/ŌłÜ5 = 7/ŌłÜ5 = 7ŌłÜ5/5.

questions.question: A surveyor marks corners of a triangular garden at A(0,0), B(12,0) and C(6,8) metres. Calculate the exact perimeter of the garden.
